title: K.A.A. Gent

infobox.value: 1864; 162 years ago (1864) (as a gymnastics association)  1900; 126 years ago (1900) (as a football association)

(Dutch pronunciation: [[ˈkoːnɪŋkləkə ʔɑtləˈtik ɑsoːˈɕaːsi ˈɣɛnt]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch"); English: *Royal Athletic Association Ghent*), often simply known as **Gent** or by their nickname ***De Buffalo's*** (English: The Buffalos), is a Belgian professional [sports club](/wiki/Sports_club "Sports club"), based in the city of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders"). Their [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") team is the best known section within the club and has been playing in the [Belgian First Division A](/wiki/Belgian_First_Division_A "Belgian First Division A") since the [1989–90 season](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_Belgian_First_Division "1989–90 Belgian First Division"). They won the national league once, in [2014–15](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Belgian_Pro_League "2014–15 Belgian Pro League"), in addition to four [Belgian Cup](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup") victories. Gent played their home matches in the [Jules Ottenstadion](/wiki/Jules_Ottenstadion "Jules Ottenstadion") in [Gentbrugge](/wiki/Gentbrugge "Gentbrugge") from 1920 until 2013, when they moved to the [Ghelamco Arena](/wiki/Ghelamco_Arena "Ghelamco Arena"). Their team colours are blue and white. The principal sponsor is Baloise, with Hamann International Logistics sponsoring for the [UEFA Conference League](/wiki/UEFA_Conference_League "UEFA Conference League") matches.

(and it is still referred to as such in the French-speaking part of Belgium). They changed their name to the current Dutch version in 1971. The football division opened in 1900. The nickname of the club is *De Buffalo's*, a term coined after a visit of the original [Buffalo Bill](/wiki/Buffalo_Bill "Buffalo Bill") and his [Wild West circus](/wiki/Wild_West_shows "Wild West shows") to the city in the early 20th century.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Their logo features a [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") in profile. The origins of this logo are attributed to the feather headdresses worn by the Indian chiefs who performed with Buffalo Bill, though this logo has [garnered some controversy](/wiki/Native_American_mascot_controversy "Native American mascot controversy") in recent years.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [K.A.A. Gent](/w/index.php?title=K.A.A._Gent&redirect=no "K.A.A. Gent"))

Belgian professional football club

For the women's team, see [KAA Gent (women)](/wiki/KAA_Gent_(women) "KAA Gent (women)").

Not to be confused with [KRC Genk](/wiki/KRC_Genk "KRC Genk").

**Koninklijke Atletiek Associatie Gent** (Dutch pronunciation: [[ˈkoːnɪŋkləkə ʔɑtləˈtik ɑsoːˈɕaːsi ˈɣɛnt]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch"); English: *Royal Athletic Association Ghent*), often simply known as **Gent** or by their nickname ***De Buffalo's*** (English: The Buffalos), is a Belgian professional [sports club](/wiki/Sports_club "Sports club"), based in the city of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders"). Their [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") team is the best known section within the club and has been playing in the [Belgian First Division A](/wiki/Belgian_First_Division_A "Belgian First Division A") since the [1989–90 season](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_Belgian_First_Division "1989–90 Belgian First Division"). They won the national league once, in [2014–15](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Belgian_Pro_League "2014–15 Belgian Pro League"), in addition to four [Belgian Cup](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup") victories. Gent played their home matches in the [Jules Ottenstadion](/wiki/Jules_Ottenstadion "Jules Ottenstadion") in [Gentbrugge](/wiki/Gentbrugge "Gentbrugge") from 1920 until 2013, when they moved to the [Ghelamco Arena](/wiki/Ghelamco_Arena "Ghelamco Arena"). Their team colours are blue and white. The principal sponsor is Baloise, with Hamann International Logistics sponsoring for the [UEFA Conference League](/wiki/UEFA_Conference_League "UEFA Conference League") matches.

The field hockey and track and field divisions were founded in 1864, making it one of the oldest sports clubs in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). The club was then known under its French name **La Gantoise** (and it is still referred to as such in the French-speaking part of Belgium). They changed their name to the current Dutch version in 1971. The football division opened in 1900. The nickname of the club is *De Buffalo's*, a term coined after a visit of the original [Buffalo Bill](/wiki/Buffalo_Bill "Buffalo Bill") and his [Wild West circus](/wiki/Wild_West_shows "Wild West shows") to the city in the early 20th century.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Their logo features a [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") in profile. The origins of this logo are attributed to the feather headdresses worn by the Indian chiefs who performed with Buffalo Bill, though this logo has [garnered some controversy](/wiki/Native_American_mascot_controversy "Native American mascot controversy") in recent years.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Gent enjoyed its first spell at the highest level in Belgian football between [1913–14](/wiki/1913%E2%80%9314_Belgian_First_Division "1913–14 Belgian First Division") and [1928–29](/wiki/1928%E2%80%9329_Belgian_First_Division "1928–29 Belgian First Division"), and a second one from [1936–37](/wiki/1936%E2%80%9337_Belgian_First_Division "1936–37 Belgian First Division") to [1966–67](/wiki/1966%E2%80%9367_Belgian_First_Division "1966–67 Belgian First Division"). In the 1970s and 1980s, the club had several promotions and relegations between the first and [second](/wiki/Belgian_Second_Division "Belgian Second Division") divisions, before returning to the highest level in 1989. The club reached the quarter-finals of the [1991–92 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_UEFA_Cup "1991–92 UEFA Cup"), which is their best achievement ever in European competitions.

Aside from [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), Gent also have other sports sections in [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") and [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KAA_Gent&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KAA_Gent&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

In 1864, an association called the 'Société Gymnastique la Gantoise', which was tasked with promoting gymnastics, was founded. Some branches quickly became independent and in 1891 the team merged with the Association Athlétique, which was in itself a merger of younger teams, such as Racing Club, Running Club and Red Star. The new merger team was called Association Athlétique La Gantoise, and aside from gymnastics, the activities were broadened to athletics, boxing, cricket, cycling, fencing, hockey, swimming and tennis. In this context, the athletics team KAA Gent was founded.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/K.A.A.GentOldLogo%28ARAG%29.png/250px-K.A.A.GentOldLogo%28ARAG%29.png)](/wiki/File:K.A.A.GentOldLogo(ARAG).png)

1914 logo of *La Gantoise*

In the last decade of the 19th century, organized football was introduced in Ghent. Different small teams were founded and some merged into Racing Club Gantois on 1 April 1899, which would later become the biggest challenger of KAA Gent. Only in 1900, a football section was founded by the students of the College of Melle, which is a place close to Ghent. The first president of the team was doctor Hector Priem. The games were played on the Carpentierplein, which was situated at the crossroads of the Kortrijksesteenweg, the Clementinalaan, the Oostendestraat and the Astridlaan. Initially, the colours black and white were chosen, but by 31 October 1900, when the team became an official member, the colours were changed to blue and white. On 15 November 1900, the first regular game was played, against Omnium Sporting Club.

### Early 20th Century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KAA_Gent&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early 20th Century")]

For the first few years, the team mostly played in the [Belgian Second Division](/wiki/Belgian_Second_Division "Belgian Second Division"), and later on in the [First Division](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League"). In 1904 the team moved to the Mussenstraat. In 1913, the World Exposition was held at that place, and the team moved once more, this time to the Albertlaan. Over there, a football pitch, training fields, tennis courts, an athletics court, galleries and other accommodations were being built. At 9 December 1915, during the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the stadium completely burned down. In 1912–13, AA La Gantoise became champion in the Second Division. In 1914, the team received the royal title and was called Association Royale Athlétique La Gantoise, which was abbreviated to ARA La Gantoise. During the world exposition, the team organized several sporting events. The first season in the First League, 1913–14, was nevertheless very difficult for the team and only by means of a test match against [Standard Club Liégois](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège"), relegation was avoided.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1920, the team moved again, this time to Gentbrugge, where the [Jules Ottenstadion](/wiki/Jules_Ottenstadion "Jules Ottenstadion") was built. La Gantoise fell back to the Second Division and it was not until 1936 it managed to win the promotion play-offs and return to the [First Division](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League").[[7]](#cite_note-7) In the mid-fifties, the team played their strongest football yet. In 1953–54 it ended third with an equal total of points as [KFC Malinois](/wiki/KV_Mechelen "KV Mechelen") and only one point behind the champions [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht"). The next season, La Gantoise was alone on the second spot, this time with three points less than the champions.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 1964 it won the Belgian Cup ([Beker van België](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup")), which was the first major tournament victory for the team. Because of their cup win, it became the first Belgian team to participate in the European Cup Winners' Cup. La Gantoise was defeated in the first round by [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.").[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 1967, the club relegated once more, after three decades of playing in the First Division. It did, however, only take them one year to clinch promotion again.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Late 20th Century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KAA_Gent&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Late 20th Century")]

In 1971, the name of the team was translated into Flemish, as it became "Koninklijke Atletiek Associatie Gent" (commonly known as KAA Gent or AA Gent). The 1970–71 season was the start of a bad decade for Gent. They were relegated to the [Second Division](/wiki/Belgian_Second_Division "Belgian Second Division") six games before the season's ending, after the defeat to [Club Brugge](/wiki/Club_Brugge_KV "Club Brugge KV"). In 1974, they even relegated to the [Third Division](/wiki/Belgian_Third_Division "Belgian Third Division"). Gent had ended last and couldn't assure its promotion to the Second Division in the final round.[[11]](#cite_note-11) After one season, they would return to the Second Division and remained there until 1980, when the team returned to the First Division.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The 1980s would become a much better period for the team. In 1984 they won the Belgian Cup again, and during that period the team played in European competitions four times.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 1986–87, Gent reached the Third round in the UEFA Cup.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Gentbrugge_Jules_Ottenstadion_19-09-2010_18-36-08.JPG/250px-Gentbrugge_Jules_Ottenstadion_19-09-2010_18-36-08.JPG)](/wiki/File:Gentbrugge_Jules_Ottenstadion_19-09-2010_18-36-08.JPG)

During a 2010 game against [SV Zulte Waregem](/wiki/SV_Zulte_Waregem "SV Zulte Waregem")

In 1990–91, the team played at the top of the standings for a long time, under the guidance of René Vandereycken and players such as Frank Dauwen, [Eric Viscaal](/wiki/Eric_Viscaal "Eric Viscaal") and [Erwin Vandenbergh](/wiki/Erwin_Vandenbergh "Erwin Vandenbergh"), but finally it ended on the third spot. So instead of competing in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), the team played in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") in 1991. After defeating [Lausanne-Sport](/wiki/FC_Lausanne-Sport "FC Lausanne-Sport"), [Eintracht Frankfurt](/wiki/Eintracht_Frankfurt "Eintracht Frankfurt") and [Dynamo Moscow](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Moscow "FC Dynamo Moscow"), Gent played the quarter finals against [Ajax](/wiki/AFC_Ajax "AFC Ajax").[[14]](#cite_note-14) The following years, Gent fell back to the lower places in the standings. From 1994 until 1997, they finished just above the relegation places in the league.[[15]](#cite_note-15) By the end of the 1990s the results improved again, and with coach [Trond Sollied](/wiki/Trond_Sollied "Trond Sollied"), KAA Gent qualified for European football once more in 1999–00.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In these series, Gent lost heavily against [Ajax](/wiki/AFC_Ajax "AFC Ajax"), under new coach [Henk Houwaart](/wiki/Henk_Houwaart "Henk Houwaart"). The next season, Gent reached the [UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "UEFA Intertoto Cup"), where they would reach the semi-finals against [PSG](/wiki/Paris_Saint-Germain_F.C. "Paris Saint-Germain F.C."). The following seasons, league results varied between lower sub-top places and top four finishes.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KAA_Gent&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2000s")]

In 2004, Gent signed coach [Georges Leekens](/wiki/Georges_Leekens "Georges Leekens"). In his first season, the team ended at the sixth spot in competition. With Leekens as a coach, KAA Gent made some impressive performances, such as the 4–1 victory over rival [Club Brugge](/wiki/Club_Brugge_KV "Club Brugge KV") on 1 April 2006. In 2006–07, despite a weak start of the competition, the team managed to reach the fourth place in the [Belgian Pro League](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League"). It repeated that achievement the following year.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The next season, coach Georges Leekens left the club and joined [Lokeren](/wiki/K.S.C._Lokeren_Oost-Vlaanderen "K.S.C. Lokeren Oost-Vlaanderen"). [Trond Sollied](/wiki/Trond_Sollied "Trond Sollied"), the Norwegian trainer who had been very successful seven years before, succeeded him. Under his guidance, KAA Gent played its third Cup Final, in which it only lost at the end from [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht"). Sollied left Gent again after one season, this time for [Heerenveen](/wiki/SC_Heerenveen "SC Heerenveen").[[19]](#cite_note-19) [Michel Preud'homme](/wiki/Michel_Preud%27homme "Michel Preud'homme"), who had just become champion of the Jupiler Pro League with [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège"), signed a contract for three seasons, together with his colleagues [Manu Ferrera](/wiki/Manu_Ferrera "Manu Ferrera") and [Stan van den Buys](/wiki/Stan_van_den_Buys "Stan van den Buys"). In 2008–09, the team ended at the fourth spot, after a strong comeback in the second part of the competition, with an equal number of points as [Club Brugge](/wiki/Club_Brugge_KV "Club Brugge KV"), who had won one more game and ended third.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In 2009–10, there was a heavy battle for second place in the [Belgian Pro League](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League") between Gent and Club Brugge and the [Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") ticket that came with it. They played each other on 8 May 2010. Gent won by a convincing 6–2 score to earn Champions League football.[[21]](#cite_note-21) One week later, Gent also won the Belgian Cup for the first time in 26 years, defeating the other Bruges [Pro League](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League") team, [Cercle Brugge](/wiki/Cercle_Brugge_K.S.V. "Cercle Brugge K.S.V.").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KAA_Gent&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2010s")]

On 17 July 2013, the club officially inaugurated their new stadium, the [Ghelamco Arena](/wiki/Ghelamco_Arena "Ghelamco Arena"), with a 2–0 win over [VfB Stuttgart](/wiki/VfB_Stuttgart "VfB Stuttgart") in a gala match.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

On 21 May 2015, Gent clinched their first [Belgium League](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League") title by defeating [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège") 2–0 at home, automatically qualifying for the [group stage](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_UEFA_Champions_League_group_stage "2015–16 UEFA Champions League group stage") of the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League").[[24]](#cite_note-24) Gent were drawn in Group H, against Russian champions [Zenit Saint Petersburg](/wiki/FC_Zenit_Saint_Petersburg "FC Zenit Saint Petersburg"), the Spanish team [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF") and the French [Lyon](/wiki/Olympique_Lyonnais "Olympique Lyonnais"). On matchday 1, Gent draw 1–1 with Olympique Lyon at [Ghelamco Arena](/wiki/Ghelamco_Arena "Ghelamco Arena"), securing their first point in the Champions League group stages. In matchday 2, they were beaten by Zenit 1–2 at [Petrovsky Stadium](/wiki/Petrovsky_Stadium "Petrovsky Stadium"), [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"). On matchday 3, they lost again 1–2 against Valencia at [Mestalla](/wiki/Mestalla "Mestalla"), [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia "Valencia"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"). On matchday 4, at [Ghelamco Arena](/wiki/Ghelamco_Arena "Ghelamco Arena"), Gent defeated Valencia 1–0, after [Sven Kums](/wiki/Sven_Kums "Sven Kums") successfully converted a penalty kick in the second half to obtain their first Champions League victory. On matchday 5, at [Stade de Gerland](/wiki/Stade_de_Gerland "Stade de Gerland"), [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), Gent beat Lyon 2–1 with a dramatic winning goal with the last touch of the match in the 95th minute. On matchday 6, Gent won 2–1 against Zenit, finishing the group in second place and becoming only the second Belgian team to advance to the Champions League knockout phase after Anderlecht in [2000–01](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_UEFA_Champions_League "2000–01 UEFA Champions League"). In the round of 16, Gent were drawn against [Wolfsburg](/wiki/VfL_Wolfsburg "VfL Wolfsburg"). In the first leg at Ghelamco Stadium, Gent were defeated 2–3 by Wolfsburg. The second game, this time in Wolfsburg, ended 1–0, ending Gent's European tournament. However, it was the best European season for them.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 2016–17 season, Gent played in the Europa League. They faced [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C."), first winning at home in the [Ghelamco Arena](/wiki/Ghelamco_Arena "Ghelamco Arena") and then drawing in [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium"), thus advancing on aggregate. Around 8,000 KAA Gent fans attended the match in the away-end, after they were awarded an extra 1,000 tickets for their excellent reputation.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) In the next round they were defeated by fellow Belgian side KRC Genk.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KAA_Gent&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

KAA Gent have a fierce rivalry with [Club Brugge](/wiki/Club_Brugge_KV "Club Brugge KV"), in what is dubbed as the "Battle of [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders")" in the media[[27]](#cite_note-27) as it is between Flanders' two cultural capitals (Antwerp having been historically a part of the [Duchy of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant")). There are also many Club Brugge supporters in the city of Ghent due to internal migration from [West Flanders](/wiki/West_Flanders "West Flanders") to the city, while KAA Gent pride themselves on their local identity. The nickname that KAA Gent fans give to the Club Brugge fans is the Dutch word "boeren" ("peasants"),[[28]](#cite_note-28) mainly because of the agricultural background of West-Flanders but also because of the insolence that Club Brugge fans have displayed in the past.

## Well-known former players of the team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KAA_Gent&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Well-known former players of the team")]

Six players of AA Gent held top scorer positions in the [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"): [Maurice Willems](/wiki/Maurice_Willems "Maurice Willems") (1956–57, 28 games, 35 goals), [Ronny Martens](/wiki/Ronny_Martens "Ronny Martens") (1984–85, 34 games, 23 goals), [Erwin Vandenbergh](/wiki/Erwin_Vandenbergh "Erwin Vandenbergh") (1990–91, 34 games, 23 goals) and [Ole Martin Arst](/wiki/Ole_Martin_Arst "Ole Martin Arst") (1999–00, 33 games, 30 goals), [Jonathan David](/wiki/Jonathan_David "Jonathan David") (2019–20, 29 games, 18 goals), [Hugo Cuypers](/wiki/Hugo_Cuypers "Hugo Cuypers") (2022–23, 39 games, 27 goals).

The Belgian player [Roland Storme](/wiki/Roland_Storme "Roland Storme"), central defender of KAA Gent in 1958–59, received the Golden Shoe award. Three other AA Gent players were presented with awards and honors: [René Vandereycken](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Vandereycken "René Vandereycken") got the award for trainer of the year 1991. [Frédéric Herpoel](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Herpoel "Frédéric Herpoel") was chosen as the best goalkeeper in 2004.

[Mbark Boussoufa](/wiki/Mbark_Boussoufa "Mbark Boussoufa") received multiple awards and honors including: pro-player of the year, best young player and the award of the 12th man, as well as the [Ebony Shoe](/wiki/Ebony_Shoe_Award "Ebony Shoe Award"). Another AA Gent player, the Egyptian [Ahmed "Mido" Hossam](/wiki/Mido_(footballer) "Mido (footballer)") was also presented with the Ebony Shoe 8 years earlier in 2001.

[Maurice Willems](/wiki/Maurice_Willems "Maurice Willems") has scored more goals than any other KAA Gent player, with 185 goals between 1952 and 1962.

[Armand Seghers](/wiki/Armand_Seghers "Armand Seghers") holds the record of the most games played in the first team of KAA Gent: 507 between 1949 and 1960.

[Marc Van Der Linden](/wiki/Marc_Van_Der_Linden "Marc Van Der Linden") was in the national selection of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") for the [1990 World Cup](/wiki/1990_World_Cup "1990 World Cup") in Italy.

[Richard Orlans](/wiki/Richard_Orlans "Richard Orlans") holds the most selections for the [Belgium National Team](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team"), more than any other KAA Gent player. He was selected 21 times from 1955 – 1958.

[Frédéric Herpoel](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Herpoel "Frédéric Herpoel") was four times honoured with the [Jean-Claude Bouvy Trophy](/wiki/Jean-Claude_Bouvy_Trophy "Jean-Claude Bouvy Trophy") for "most valuable player of the season" between 2002 – 2005.

[Tore André Dahlum](/wiki/Tore_Andr%C3%A9_Dahlum "Tore André Dahlum") was a Norwegian international who played one year for Gent.

Congolese player [Leon Mokuna](/wiki/Leon_Mokuna "Leon Mokuna") was the first African player in Belgian competition, in 1957. Compatriot [Pierre Mwana Kasongo](/wiki/Pierre_Mwana_Kasongo "Pierre Mwana Kasongo") would join the club in 1965 and [Kiyika Tokodi](/wiki/Kiyika_Tokodi "Kiyika Tokodi") would do so in 1980.

## Jean-Claude Bouvy Trophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=KAA_Gent&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Jean-Claude Bouvy Trophy")]

The **Jean-Claude Bouvy Trophy** is an award that is annually handed out to the most valuable player of [Belgian](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club K.A.A. Gent. It was established in 1979 and later named after Jean-Claude Bouvy, a player of Gent who died in a car crash in 1986.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
